title: Don’t repeat post-Cold War error: RBA
full_text:
Australia’s financial institutions are war-gaming the rising threat of geopolitical shocks, including cyber attacks, says Reserve Bank assistant governor Brad Jones, who warns that financial markets are showing the sort of complacency similar to that of the post-Cold War era.
Challenges to “international cohesion” had re-emerged for a number of years, leading countries to revisit security postures and increase defence spending, Dr Jones said in a speech on Wednesday to the Australian Banking Association Conference.
“Some indicators suggest a fracturing is occurring on a scale and with a speed unseen in eight decades, others less so,” he said.
The sense of certainty that pervaded international affairs following the end of the Cold War, and that “greased the wheels” of global finance and trade had perhaps been “the first casualty” in the heightened security environment, he said.
As assistant governor for financial systems, Dr Jones said there needed to be a focus on stronger cyber security. “Two key developments are tilting the offensive-defensive balance in favour of offensive cyber capabilities: a widening attack surface as more activity becomes digital; and the pitting of state-backed resources against those of private enterprise,” he said.
This year there have been unprecedented warnings from the Five Eyes intelligence alliance including ASIO, MI5, the FBI, CSIS, and NZSIS, about cyber attacks. The most recent warning from the alliance earlier this month was an alert that Chinese military intelligence was aggressively using mainstream platforms like LinkedIn, Indeed, and Upwork to recruit Western targets.
Dr Jones said the increased geopolitical risks had led to heightened awareness from financial institutions.
“It is in this context that policy makers are dialling up efforts to ensure the financial system can weather a more challenging risk environment,” Dr Jones said.
“This has been a central focus of the Council of Financial Regulators for some years.” The geopolitical risks could have “first-order implications for the financial system” by undermining the resilience of critical payments infrastructure, threatening key institutions through “disinformation campaigns and insider and foreign interference”; fragmenting cross-border capital flows, increasing evasion of sophisticated sanctions and weakening the public-private co-ordination challenges in strengthening the financial safety net at home and abroad.
“The punchline is this: it is in our collective interest to prepare for a financial system that is more shock-prone,” Dr Jones said.
“There needs to be contingency planning under more extreme scenarios, more demanding fire drills, more intrusive interrogation of third-party dependencies, and more robust continuity and recoverability arrangements.” He warned of complacency, saying: “A common perspective forged from the benign geopolitical environment of the post-Cold War period was that geopolitics had become too abstract, or too marginal, to really matter for the financial system. The low-risk premiums observed in recent years across global markets are just the latest example.”
